common_name: Unicorn Monstera (True Swiss Cheese Plant, Obliqua Monstera, Ghost Monstera)

planting.note: Grow in bright indirect light in very well-draining, chunky, highly aerated aroid mix; true Monstera obliqua is one of the rarest Monsteras and most plants sold under this name in the trade are actually Monstera adansonii. Genuine M. obliqua has leaves where the fenestrations (holes) exceed the remaining leaf tissue in area — the leaf appears to be more hole than leaf. Requires high humidity (70%+), warm temperatures (65–85°F), and a climbing support. Very slow-growing. Propagate from stem cuttings. All parts toxic if ingested.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to medium indirect light in chunky, well-aerated aroid potting mix with a climbing support — true Monstera obliqua is an extremely slow-growing, delicate climbing aroid that requires consistent warmth, high humidity, and careful watering to thrive. Monstera obliqua is a rare, hemi-epiphytic climbing aroid native to Panama, Peru, Bolivia, Ecuador, Colombia, Venezuela, and Brazil in the Araceae family, one of the most coveted and least commonly available plants in the tropical collector world. The reason for its rarity and fascination is its extraordinary leaf fenestration pattern: true M. obliqua produces thin, delicate leaves in which the holes (fenestrations) are so large and numerous that they actually occupy more area than the remaining green leaf tissue — the leaf is literally more hole than leaf, a lace-like structure of the most extreme fenestration of any Monstera species. This quality makes the true plant fragile, photosensitive, and extremely challenging to cultivate compared to the robust Monstera adansonii that is frequently sold as 'M. obliqua' in the plant trade. Collectors who obtain true M. obliqua consider it one of the pinnacles of the aroid hobby. Important note: the vast majority of plants labeled 'Monstera obliqua' in commercial horticulture are actually Monstera adansonii, which is more robust and has much less fenestration. All parts of true M. obliqua are toxic.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Monstera obliqua species profile and trade confusion
International Aroid Society — Monstera obliqua vs. M. adansonii: identification guide
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Climbing Monstera species for tropical collections
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Monstera (toxic to pets)

common_name: Alligator Root (Renealmia, Vanilla-Leaf Ginger, Wild Ginger Lily)

planting.note: Plant rhizomes or container-grown clumps in late spring in partial to full shade with consistently moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil; Renealmia alpinia grows naturally as a forest understory plant and performs best in the filtered light beneath large trees. In zone 10-11, it is a dependable, spreading perennial; in zone 9, treat as a container plant. The plant produces stems with large, dark-green, lance-shaped leaves with a vanilla-like fragrance when crushed. The showy inflorescences bear red-orange flowers followed by bright red to orange-scarlet berries. Propagate by rhizome division.

directions: Plant in partial to full shade in consistently moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil — Renealmia alpinia thrives in the shaded, moist conditions of a tropical understory garden and wilts and declines in exposed, dry, or overly sunny positions. Renealmia alpinia is a clump-forming, rhizomatous tropical perennial native to the Caribbean Islands, Mexico, Central America, and northern South America in the family Zingiberaceae (the ginger family), related to Alpinia and Zingiber. The plant produces tall (4–8 foot), cane-like, leafy stems clothed in large, lance-shaped to oblong, dark-green leaves that, when crushed, release a pleasant fragrance combining vanilla, ginger, and green herbs — a quality that has led to the plant's use in traditional folk medicine and cooking throughout its native range, where the aromatic leaves, rhizomes, and berries flavor foods and beverages. The inflorescences emerge near the base of the plant on short separate stems — a basal inflorescence style common in Renealmia — bearing showy clusters of orange-red to red flowers followed by decorative, bright red to scarlet berries (capsules) that are genuinely ornamental. The berries are used medicinally and as a condiment in parts of Colombia and Venezuela. An unusual and attractive shade-garden ginger relative for zone 10-11 tropical gardens.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Renealmia species for Florida shade gardens
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Renealmia alpinia cultivation notes
Missouri Botanical Garden — Renealmia alpinia species profile
International Society for Horticultural Science — Renealmia: ornamental Zingiberales

common_name: Singapore Rhododendron (Indian Rhododendron, Senduduk, Malabar Melastome)

planting.note: Plant in full sun to partial shade in moist, well-drained, acidic to slightly acidic soil; Melastoma malabathricum grows vigorously in acidic, moist soils and will tolerate temporarily wet conditions better than many ornamental shrubs. It is half-hardy and can be damaged by frost but often regrows from the root crown in zone 9. In zone 9b-10, it grows as a reliable, free-flowering evergreen shrub blooming nearly continuously through the warm months. The berries turn the mouth and teeth temporarily black-purple when eaten (the name 'Melastoma' means black mouth). Has become invasive in some Pacific Islands and Hawaii — check local regulations.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in moist, acidic, well-drained soil — Singapore Rhododendron produces the most flowers in full sun but tolerates and grows attractively in partial shade, which suits its natural habitat as an edge and forest-margin species. Melastoma malabathricum is a fast-growing, evergreen, multi-stemmed tropical shrub native from India and Sri Lanka through all of Southeast Asia to Australia in the Melastomataceae family, widely grown throughout the tropical world as an ornamental flowering shrub for its prolific production of bright, showy, 5-petaled flowers in vivid magenta-pink to rose-purple. The flowers are large for a shrub (2–3 inches across), saucer-shaped, with five distinctly veined petals in intense magenta to hot pink and a central cluster of curved, two-toned stamens in yellow and purple — a flower with genuine ornamental impact. The plant blooms throughout the year in zone 10-11, never without flowers for long. The common name 'Singapore Rhododendron' reflects both its wide use as an ornamental in Singapore and the general resemblance of the flower to a tropical Rhododendron, though the plants are not botanically related. The ripe berries are edible (though they turn the mouth and teeth temporarily black — the genus name means 'black mouth' in Greek), sweet, and consumed in Southeast Asian traditional practice. A robust, reliable, and free-flowering ornamental shrub for zone 9b-11 warm, moist gardens.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Melastoma malabathricum for Florida landscapes
National Parks Board Singapore — Melastoma malabathricum native range and cultivation
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Tropical flowering shrubs for Gulf Coast zone 9-10
LSU AgCenter — Tropical Ornamental Shrubs for Gulf Coast Louisiana Zone 10

common_name: Queensland Trumpet Tree (Deplanchea, Four-Leaf Deplanchea, Yellow Cassod Tree)

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in late spring in full sun with well-drained, sandy to moderately fertile soil; Deplanchea tetraphylla is frost-sensitive and suited to zone 10-11 outdoor planting. The species name 'tetraphylla' (four-leaved) refers to the arrangement of large leaves in whorls of four at each node — a distinctive feature. The massive terminal flower clusters are bright orange-yellow and spectacular — plant where they can be viewed from a distance. Relatively fast-growing for a tropical tree, reaching 30–40 feet at maturity. The tree grows naturally at forest margins and in disturbed forest edges.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile soil — Queensland Trumpet Tree grows most vigorously and produces its best flower displays in full sun conditions. Deplanchea tetraphylla is a medium to large, evergreen tropical tree native to northeastern Queensland, Australia (Cape York Peninsula and adjacent Queensland rainforest margins) in the Bignoniaceae family, one of the outstanding ornamental flowering trees of northeastern Australia and one of the least known outside the botanical garden world. The tree has a distinctive, immediately recognizable appearance due to its whorled leaf arrangement: the large, broadly oval to obovate leaves (12–18 inches long) are arranged in precise whorls of four at each node rather than the alternating or opposite arrangement of most trees — giving the tree an architectural, tiered, symmetrical appearance. The flowers are spectacular: large, dense, terminal panicles of bright orange-yellow, tubular flowers crowded into massive heads at the branch tips, each individual flower about 1 inch long with five spreading lobes, produced in profuse display in spring to summer. The orange-yellow flower clusters at the tips of the whorled branches create a striking visual pattern — concentric rings of color and foliage. A rare but genuinely impressive zone 10-12 ornamental flowering tree that deserves wider planting in tropical and subtropical botanical gardens and large residential landscapes.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Deplanchea tetraphylla species profile
Queensland Government — Deplanchea tetraphylla native range and ecology
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Australian tropical trees for South Florida
Floridata Plant Encyclopedia — Bignoniaceae ornamental trees

common_name: Carajuru (Chica Vine, Blood Vine, Bejuco de Indio)

planting.note: Plant container-grown vines in late spring on a strong trellis or arbor in full sun to partial shade; Fridericia chica (formerly Bignonia chica, Arrabidaea chica) is a vigorous tropical vine suited to zone 10-11 outdoor culture. It grows rapidly and blooms profusely in the warm months with clusters of rosy-pink to magenta trumpet flowers. The leaves have been used for millennia as a source of a red-orange to brick-red natural dye ('chica' dye) by indigenous peoples of the Amazon Basin for body painting and ceremonial use. The dye color comes from the carotenoid pigment bixin. Propagate from cuttings.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, moderately fertile soil — Carajuru blooms most prolifically in full sun and produces attractive foliage display in partial shade. Fridericia chica (formerly Arrabidaea chica, Bignonia chica) is a vigorous, woody, tendril-climbing vine native to Mexico, Central America, the Caribbean, and tropical South America (Peru, Brazil, Colombia, Venezuela) in the Bignoniaceae family — one of the most historically significant ornamental plants of the tropical Americas for a quality beyond its ornamental flower display: its leaves yield 'carajuru' or 'chica,' a brilliant red-orange to brick-red natural dye that has been used by indigenous peoples of the Amazon Basin for body decoration, face painting, textile dyeing, and ceremonial preparation for thousands of years, and that remains in use today. The vine produces clusters of attractive, rosy-pink to magenta-pink, 1.5-inch, tubular trumpet flowers with five spreading petal lobes, produced through the warm months and attracting hummingbirds. The compound leaves (2 leaflets + tendril) contain the carotenoid pigment bixin and related compounds responsible for the red dye — the same class of compound that gives annatto its orange color. Beyond its ethnobotanical significance, F. chica is a fast-growing, flowering tropical vine with genuine ornamental value in zone 10-12 warm garden conditions.
sources: Missouri Botanical Garden — Fridericia chica (Arrabidaea chica) species profile
Smithsonian Tropical Research Institute — Carajuru: Amazonian plant dyes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Bignoniaceae vines for tropical Florida
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Tropical Bignoniaceae vines for South Florida
